speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is December the 13th, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Odette Ross in London, England. And this is tape number 2.

speaker: interviewee
text: You asked me about where we returned to live to and how I felt about my parents. Well, we went to live in my uncle's flat with them. And at that point, it never occurred to me that my parents could be anywhere else but in France. And having worked out that they crossed the frontier afterwards, my brother and I felt sure that they were also caught by the Germans. So we waited desperately for them to come back, and I used to wait at the end of a tram line every night to see whether my parents were on the, on the tram or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 13-- yep. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At one stage, before we were caught by the Germans, we had the opportunity near [NON-ENGLISH] to catch a, catch a boat which would take refugees to England. My uncle and aunt and everybody was very keen. I think this is, this is the-- I know it's true, but I'm saying, well, whether I was the greatest influence, I don't know. And I remember refusing point blank to go on that ship. I said, my parents are going to go to Cognac, and I'm not going to England. We didn't get on the ship. Exactly why, I don't know. But I know that I was very relieved. Ironically, my parents, after having begged and borrowed and managed to walk across to practically Calais, by the time they had no soles on their shoes, found a Polish ship which was meant to go to Bordeaux and then along the coast of Spain and Portugal. And because my father still spoke Polish or because they heard his sad tale of the children, they allowed him onto the ship. Now, three days later of no food and terrible, terrible seas-- the Channel was mined. And my parents arrived in England, which is not what they had planned to do. They were welcomed very warmly. But by that time, they were war refugees and there was no means for them to get out of England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they arrive to when they--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the actual port. I-- I think they arrived at Harwich. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The reason I don't know is that-- I don't think my father blocked things up, but I could never ask him anything about these two years. And maybe I didn't speak about it either. It's only very, very much later [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] when, unfortunately, I had to tell my mother that her son had died and that she cried. She said that she had only cried once before and actually was a very controlled person. And that was when we were separated from the children. And she begged the, the captain to take her on the, on the boat. And she told me about the grueling journey she had. But I don't think she ever mentioned where they actually landed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As refugees, they were helped, you know, quite-- the British were very good to them. Although they were given 1 pound per family per week to live on. But they were boarded in Amersham somewhere. My father arrived in England without one penny because, again, because he was-- he had a Polish passport. He had decided that his younger brother, who had been his partner and who was Belgian, should be carrying our little fortune. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so my, my parents arrived nearly penniless. My mother had her engagement ring, which I don't think was all that valuable. I don't know. She offered to sell it, but my father wouldn't. Fortunately, he was very good at his job and he started-- again, in that business, you always seem to know someone. And someone employed him originally for 5 pounds a week. And there was a lot of movement in industrial diamonds because they were very important during the war. And certainly my father started earning a, you know, a little money. About three months after we were back in Antwerp, we listened every day to Free Belgian Radio, which gave the name of the refugees who'd arrived in England. In fact, the Dutch radio did it, the French radio, [NON-ENGLISH]. And our neighbors or friends came and said they had heard the night before, the one we didn't hear, we didn't listen, that my parents were in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: You must have been relieved.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a great relief. We, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we couldn't believe that they would be in England. But we hadn't heard that they'd got killed, and they hadn't come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had thought they might be there. We wrote via the Red Cross. But I think it took about 18 months before we got a reply to say that they, they had found them in England.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the United States was not at war. And my father had a very good friend and colleague called Jack Content. So my brother wrote a letter addressed to Jack Content, Diamond Company, New York-- we had no idea of an address whatsoever-- saying that he believes my father could possibly be-- our father could possibly be in England and could he send-- forward the letter to England. This Jack Content forwarded the letter to Emil Rubinstein Diamond [INAUDIBLE], London. And believe it or not, my father got it. And so for about 18 months, we were able to conduct some sort of correspondence via New York because America didn't enter the war until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] December of '41, I think, and by which time we had, we had left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you went back to Antwerp, did you manage to go back to school again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. At the beginning, we went back to school. The Germans were very systematic. You see, I am being objective about it because so much time has gone by. I remember the curfew and being frightened and very concerned, not knowing what was going to come next. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But I can understand them organizing everything slowly. In fact, I understand they never told the people they were going to be gassed, because they didn't want any rebellion. They wanted people to do as they were told. The curfew was established quite early on. I didn't always stick to it, because I was a girl of 13. My brother was tall for 15, and we, we knew that young Jews were being surrounded for any excuse to go to, to work camps. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he, you know, kept a low profile. But we went back to school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my uncle and aunt were in their own home, and so they had their own clothes and, and whatever. But my brother and I didn't have anything. Our school books were in our apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't-- you couldn't go back there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was decided they would have a go. So I went to the [NON-ENGLISH], which was the headquarters of the Gestapo, and asked for permission to go into my apartment. And strangely enough, they gave it to me. But they said it was-- I was the only one that would be allowed to go in. My brother wouldn't, nor would my aunt. So one fine day, we went to, to my flat. And I was sent up alone. I remember being very frightened [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but really behaving quite well. I think it was the sense of survival. There was no option. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You just did things. You didn't live. You existed. And you did things automatically in the hope that it would help you to, to [INAUDIBLE] survive. Well, I went upstairs and was greeted by a charming German naval officer who was married because he had a daughter and with a girlfriend from Belgium wearing my mother's kimono. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's not a very important detail, but I remember my father buying her this kimono.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was too good to use because it was new. But his Belgian girlfriend was wearing it. I took some clothes and took some-- I remember walking to my room and feeling as if it wasn't really happening to me. I collected my books and he said to me, come here, little girl. And he said, how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said I was 13. And he said, oh, I've got a daughter about your age at home. He said, pity you're Jewish. Because we're going to exterminate Europe of all Jews. I remember this very well. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when people tell me that the Germans didn't know, I can really prove that is not so. They may not have known about gas chambers, but they certainly knew that the policy of Germany was to get rid of the Jews some way or other. The same man accompanied me, accompanied me downstairs. And my aunt was waiting downstairs because the Gestapo wouldn't give her a permit and said to her, get out of Antwerp. Try and get out. I didn't know how aware people are of the geographical situation at the time. There were such thing as so-called unoccupied France, which was really run by Petain, Laval, and the Italians. But it was supposed to have been, been unoccupied. And in unoccupied France, there were consulates and people who tried, people who tried to escape, tried to get there, and then hoped to be able to cross, either with a visa or on foot, the Pyrenees, get to Portugal, and perhaps find freedom there. From that moment onward, my uncle and aunt started thinking about trying to get away, to get out. It was an extremely difficult decision to make, and my uncle quite understandably wasn't the, the bravest person. Life was fairly comfortable. We weren't wearing armbands yet. We didn't have our passports stamped. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there weren't a lot of raids as then. There were raids on the diamond [INAUDIBLE]. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your uncle in diamonds as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I don't think they were organized. I think there was just Germans ready to loot, because the Jews would throw their diamonds. And then the Germans would disappear. The, the raids and all the arrests, really, started in 1942. Anyway, from about July 1940 to October 1941, which is when we did leave, we talked about it and weighed the pros and cons. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We wrote to my father and explained to him the dangers-- that is, if we were caught, we would be sent to concentration camps. And the chances are that one could be caught. My father, of course, who was dying to see us again, was wise enough to say that he couldn't possibly take the responsibility for the decision. The decision would have to be made by my uncle. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had a young cousin of 20, one of whom survived, who--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who belonged to your uncle and aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. This was my father's sister's son. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because he didn't have much responsibility, he dabbled a bit in the diamond business, in black marketeering. He sold and bought bread. He belonged to a Zionist organization and altogether [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was more enterprising than perhaps my uncle who was a father of children. He-- this cousin came up to my uncle and said, I could organize a passeur, which translated is, is the word smuggler. But it isn't really a smuggler. It is just people who know, who knew how to cross the frontiers between Belgium and France. And that for 20,000 Belgian francs, they could organize our escape to unoccupied France. 20,000 Belgian francs is, in those days, is equivalent of 25,000 pounds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Per person?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was per family. Strange thing-- my cousin tells me that there were people-- for us, it was a lot of money. I didn't know what my family possessed, but it wasn't sort of all that much.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there were some people who were really very wealthy and who said, 25,000 pounds? No, nothing is going to happen to me. Look, they're not arresting us yet and this and that and the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, according to my cousin, my father took-- my uncle took the plunge only because of my brother and myself. And I hoped that we would be able to be reunited. And fortunately, as I said, he had the funds and not my father. And therefore, we were able to organize the escape. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] On the 15th of October, 1941, which is also my, my brother's birthday, we left. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- my brother was told about it. We girls weren't, but I had an inkling. First of all, it was the only thing that I thought of all the time was to try and get away and be reunited with my parents. I think a girl of 13 needs her parents perhaps more than a son. Also something which I understand now but didn't understand then-- there were three girls and one boy in that family. I got on very well with one cousin and not that well with the other. And obviously, the mother took the side of the daughter, and I, I felt neglected and unloved. In fact, in retrospect, when I realized what my aunt did for two strangers, it was really quite remarkable. But I wasn't aware of that. I was only aware of the little bit of unfairness now and again. And I missed my parents desperately. Anyway, we were told to go to bed early. But being very nosy, I heard some little goings on and I, I think I might have [INAUDIBLE]. I knew that my uncle and aunt were packing. And the next morning very early, we left for the station.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much luggage did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We each had a case. And each, each child had a case. And we went to the station where we must have met another 25 or 26 people. There was a passeur with us. And another man who nobody quite knew who he was but we think he was-- so the head of the organization. And my young cousin who came with us said to my uncle, look, I'll come with you as far as Paris to help you and give you moral support. And I suppose for these younger people, it was an experience more than anything else. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I discussed this with my cousin last week. We, the children, were carrying the diamonds in our [INAUDIBLE], not the parents. And nobody was supposed to know about it. But somehow, this man with a hat who seemed to be in charge of the escape route said to my cousin, well, if we have problems, we can always use the diamonds or something like that. My cousin said you don't touch those children. You don't go anywhere near them. Because what they could've done is taken the jacket off our shoulders or whatever. Anyway, that somehow didn't happen anyway. We got onto an ordinary train. And the frontier between Belgium and France had been changed from where it was to the Somme River, which actually makes more sense because a river is a better space for a frontier. And the frontier was renamed [NON-ENGLISH]. Why, I don't quite know. But one of the difficult things we had to do was to cross the [NON-ENGLISH]. We got onto a normal train, and we got off just before the frontier. We waited for quite a long time at the station. And then when it was dark, we all-- we were told-- like a herd, we did what we were told. We knew then that it was all or nothing. We walked for a few miles along the railroad lines. And there we stopped. At that point, we were given instructions that a certain train-- and we, we would be told which one it was-- would slow down. And a couple doors would open in cattle wagons. And this is where we had to jump onto-- that the train would be very slow but would still be moving. And that train would then take us to Paris. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The French railway workers, by the way, were very good. A lot of them were members of the resistance. But they liked the money as well. So although they were paid, it was a mixture of both. They, they would be helping-- I say the Jews because I think mainly Jews tried to get out. There were some non-Jews. But they were really quite pleasant. Anyway, this said train arrived and slowed down. And we were given the signals, and we jumped on. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, it was a cattle wagon like the wagons that the people were taken to concentration camp, except we were going to freedom. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But whenever I see these, I, I can only think not of myself but the people being--

speaker: interviewer
text: Transported.

speaker: interviewee
text: --carted to the camps in those cattle wagons. And it was dark. It was in the middle of the night. And one man-- as I was on the train-- sort of cried help. And I remember pulling him. And when I got him onto the train, he said, you know, you saved my life. I, I didn't understand what he was saying. But basically, he had a gammy leg. And his leg had been caught under the, sort of the step. And I did pull him up. And then I think we stayed in complete silence until we arrived in Paris in the early morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that take? Quite a few hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took about an hour and a half. Because, you know-- I mean, now, you can actually go back from Antwerp to Paris in two and a half hours. Admittedly, the, the trains were slower then. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Paris with the passeur, we asked the big guy with the hat who wanted, who was interested in our diamonds-- I don't know whether he went back or not. And my cousin went back. We were met by other passeurs, who took us to a home. The home was run by poor Jews-- this is quite coincidental-- who gave us breakfast and made us very welcome. But I, I think that they were also being paid. Because it was such a poor, little home. In fact, there were 20 or so of us, and I think we were dispersed in two or three homes. We spent a night there to, to relax and gather our strength. And then we were greeted by a French passeur, who told us what our next step would be. The next step was to cross the frontier between occupied France and unoccupied France. And that was called the [NON-ENGLISH], the demarcation line. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were told that we would be taking a train to Navarre because there were no customs or no problem within occupied France up to Navarre. Navarre was in the frontier. There, we would be met by, again, other people who would tell us what to do. We did this, and we got to Navarre quite late in the evening. I somehow have a blank there. I can't remember what we did for a few hours. But I knew that we were told we would be crossing the frontier at daybreak. And we would be walking for about two or three hours. And we would have to do this in compete silence because there were Germans and German dogs. But if we followed them very silently and not too near-- I remember we sort of-- we more or less walked in a line. And although it was October, the weather wasn't bad. We duly did this. We heard, heard the German dogs. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I suppose we were just numb at that, at that stage. We just-- we never discussed our fear, our anguish, our hopes. We just did what we wanted to do and we were told. And after about three hours, we arrived in unoccupied France. There, we were greeted by some charming, lovely French farmers, who pushed their arms on us and welcomed us. And when we got there, all they had to offer us was some very sour and cheap vino. I was about-- I was 13 or-- no, I was 14. What I wanted was a glass of milk or a cup of tea or coffee. And it didn't put me off for life, but it put me off of wine for a very long time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Our destinations, according to the passeur, was Grenoble. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We actually wanted to go to Nice, which was nicknamed the refugees' town because there were consulates there. But we were told to go to Grenoble because there, we would be given ration cards. I think French-- [INAUDIBLE] French [INAUDIBLE]. I'm not sure about that. And there's no one I can ask anymore because they're dead. But I know that we had ration cards. In Grenoble, we went to a little hotel called [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know-- I presume-- you know, we-- what we paid to get across did not include the cost of this little hotel. It was a bitterly cold winter. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my brother and I knew at that time that there was a visa waiting for-- an entry visa waiting for us in Lisbon, Portugal because the British allowed German refugees and parents of refugees, not brothers or sisters. So really, somehow or other, we had to get across to Portugal. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We knew that people crossed the Pyrenees. And we made inquiries. They would've taken my brother but not me. I was too young. They thought I couldn't do it. And after three months, about the beginning of '42, my uncle decided to go to Nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So during that first three months you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Grenoble. It was cold and miserable. And we really-- we didn't go to school. We didn't do anything. We just waited for the next step.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year was that? Winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And it-- I remember, actually. We all got bronchitis, and we were quite ill. Eventually, we did get a doctor. One tried not to. Because I don't think that our papers were absolutely legal. And you never knew when you were going to be caught out or--

speaker: interviewer
text: That you could fully trust anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or when you were going to be denounced. Because the French were very iffy. Anyway, we moved. We took the train to Nice, early 1942, in the hope that once we got there, we would be able to get more ration books. Because without ration books, you really couldn't buy food. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Nice was nicknamed the refugee town. And then when we got there, we did meet some people we knew and even some relatives, one being an aunt of mine who is still alive today and is 102 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does she live? In England?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's now in Reno. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was a remarkable woman. And she had contact with the Germans and the Italians and helped a lot of people. But unfortunately, she had a very lovely daughter who thought that she could go backwards and forwards from occupied France to Belgium. And she was unfortunately caught by the Germans and killed in a ghastly way in a concentration camp.
